sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Encephalitis is inflammation of the brain and may result from viral, bacterial, fungal, parasitic, post-infectious, or autoimmune causes. In Western Australia, mosquito-borne causes of concern include Japanese encephalitis virus and Murray Valley encephalitis virus. Prehospital care focuses on symptom control, airway protection, seizure management, and urgent transport.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Headache
- Fever
- Decreased consciousness, confusion, or altered mental state
- Focal neurological deficits
- Seizures
- Photophobia or neck stiffness

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Rural travel, outdoor exposure, and mosquito exposure increase risk of some viral encephalitides
- Most JEV infections are asymptomatic, but severe disease can cause coma, paralysis, or permanent neurological injury
- HSV encephalitis is an important cause in Australasia and requires urgent hospital treatment
- Pregnancy may increase the significance of some infections and should lower the threshold for transfer
- Early recognition is critical; maintain a low threshold for urgent evacuation

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Insert an appropriate airway adjunct if required
- Consider Oxygen
- If actively seizing and within scope, administer seizure treatment as per protocol
- Consider simple analgesia or antipyretic only if safe and appropriate

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access
- Consider fluids if necessary
- Consider IV midazolam for seizures in line with protocol
- Consider analgesia
- Antiviral or antibiotic therapy should only be started in consultation with the on-call doctor or receiving service
- Consider ECG

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- JEV is vaccine preventable in eligible risk groups
- JEV and MVEV are notifiable diseases
